model_narrative: We route flow through a directed transportation network. Each node may inject some flow into the network from outside and may withdraw some flow to meet external requirements. Our decision is how much flow to send along each directed arc, where every unit shipped on an arc incurs a known per-unit cost. The objective is to make the total shipping cost over all arcs as small as possible.

sets.name: node
sets.doc: the nodes of the transportation network, given as integer ids

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: the directed arcs of the network, each an ordered pair of nodes where flow may travel from the first node to the second node

params.name: cost
params.doc: the unit shipping cost incurred for each unit of flow sent along an arc, in dollars per unit

params.name: capacity
params.doc: the maximum amount of flow that an arc can carry, in units

params.name: supply
params.doc: the amount of flow injected into the network from outside at a node, in units

params.name: demand
params.doc: the amount of flow withdrawn from the network to satisfy external requirements at a node, in units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of flow sent along each arc

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every arc can carry only so much flow. For each arc, the flow sent along that arc must not exceed the arc's carrying capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Flow must be conserved at every node. For each node, the flow injected into the network from outside at that node together with the total flow arriving on arcs that point into the node must equal the total flow leaving on arcs that point out of the node together with the flow withdrawn to meet external requirements at that node.

query: Suppose the corridor feeding hub 49 from the south — the 7-49, 10-49 and 14-49 legs together — gets throttled to 12000 units combined. Where does total cost land?

query: There's a proposal on the table to even out hub 34's two big feeders so it doesn't lean so hard on the trunk out of node 23. The idea is to require the lane coming in from node 20 to carry at least as much as the lane coming in from node 23. Plug that in and tell me where total routing cost lands.

query: Engineering is pitching a per-lane throttle on everything fanning out of source 21 — no single lane leaving that node should move more than 9000 units, so the load gets spread across its outbound routes instead of piling onto a couple of them. Apply that across all of node 21's outbound lanes and walk me through the resulting cost.

query: Sink 29's whole intake is basically riding on two lanes — the ones in from nodes 7 and 21 — and together they're carrying north of 24000 units. That's a lot of exposure on just two routes. Shouldn't those two lanes together stay under 21000 so the rest of the network shares the load? What's the model seeing that makes it pile so much onto that pair?

query: The trunk in from node 23 is hogging hub 34 — it's running a huge fraction of everything that arrives there. That single-lane concentration makes me nervous. Wouldn't it be safer to hold that trunk to no more than 45 percent of the total volume arriving at hub 34, counting all its inbound lanes together? What's keeping the model from spreading 34's intake around on its own?
expected_pyomo: model.share_2334 = Constraint(expr=model.x[23, 34] <= 0.45 * sum(model.x[i, j] for (i, j) in model.arc if j == 34))

query: I want to lean on source 23 less in two places at once. Couldn't we hold its trunk into hub 34 to 20000 and at the same time keep its lane into market 42 under 9000, so that node stops dominating both destinations? Walk me through what's pulling the plan toward leaning on node 23 for both of those.

query: Market 47 leans almost entirely on its two main feeders — the lanes in from nodes 21 and 14 — and between them they cover the bulk of its demand. That worries me from a resilience standpoint. Shouldn't those two feeders together be held to 14000 so the market isn't so dependent on that single pair? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making that I'm not seeing?
